name: Santo Domingo
summary: Santo Domingo is generally safe for tourists who take standard precautions, though it has the highest crime rates in the Dominican Republic. The US State Department maintains a Level 2 advisory for increased caution.
quickVerdict: Santo Domingo can be safely visited with proper precautions and awareness. While crime exists, especially petty theft and some violent crime, millions of tourists visit annually without incidents. The Zona Colonial (Colonial Zone) is well-patrolled by Tourist Police during the day but requires caution at night. Most crime affecting tourists involves pickpocketing, bag snatching, and occasional scams rather than serious violent crime. Using common sense, staying in tourist areas, and avoiding displays of wealth significantly reduce risks.

neighborhoods.name: Zona Colonial
neighborhoods.description: Historic center with UNESCO World Heritage status. Heavy tourist police presence during the day, vibrant restaurants and attractions. Becomes less safe after 6pm when it empties out.

neighborhoods.name: Piantini
neighborhoods.description: Upscale business district with good security, international restaurants, and modern amenities. One of the safest areas in the city with reliable infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Gazcue
neighborhoods.description: Middle-class residential area with embassies and museums. Generally safe during the day but requires caution on side streets and after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Cristo Rey
neighborhoods.description: Working-class neighborhood with higher crime rates. Frequent reports of robberies and gang activity. Tourists should avoid this area, especially at night.

neighborhoods.name: Villa Mella
neighborhoods.description: Northeastern district with higher poverty and crime rates. Limited police presence and frequent reports of theft. Not recommended for tourists.

neighborhoods.name: Arroyo Hondo
neighborhoods.description: Area with mixed residential and commercial zones. Higher crime rates including muggings and theft, particularly problematic after dark.

scams.name: Taxi Fare Manipulation
scams.description: Taxis don't use meters and drivers may overcharge tourists, especially from airports. Some unmarked taxis may be involved in robbery schemes.
scams.howToAvoid: Use official taxi companies like Apolo Taxi or Aero Taxi, confirm fares before starting, or use Uber which has fixed pricing.

scams.name: Credit Card and ATM Skimming
scams.description: Card skimmers at ATMs and payment terminals can steal financial information. Fake ATMs or compromised card readers target tourists.
scams.howToAvoid: Use ATMs inside banks or hotels, cover your PIN, check for unusual devices on card readers, and monitor your accounts regularly.

scams.name: Romance and Dating App Scams
scams.description: Criminals use dating apps to lure tourists to isolated locations for robbery. Victims are often taken to bars where they're drugged or overcharged.
scams.howToAvoid: Meet dates in public places, don't go to secluded locations, never leave drinks unattended, and be wary of someone pushing you to specific venues.

scams.name: Fake Police and Officials
scams.description: Criminals impersonate police officers or lawyers to extort money from tourists, often claiming document problems or traffic violations.
scams.howToAvoid: Ask for proper identification, know that real police won't demand immediate cash payments, and contact the embassy if you're suspicious of official requests.

soloFemale.overview: Solo female travelers can visit Santo Domingo safely with proper precautions, though extra vigilance is required. The city has a moderate level of safety for women, with catcalling being extremely common and constant. Tourist areas like Zona Colonial are generally safe during the day with police presence, but the city empties out after 6pm requiring careful planning for evening activities.
tips: Dress conservatively despite the heat as revealing clothing attracts unwanted attention
Use Uber or hotel-arranged taxis instead of street taxis, especially at night
Stay in well-lit, populated areas and avoid walking alone after dark
Don't display valuable jewelry, phones, or bags that could attract thieves
Trust your instincts and remove yourself from uncomfortable situations immediately

nightSafety.overview: Santo Domingo becomes significantly less safe after dark, particularly outside tourist zones. The Zona Colonial empties out around 6pm and should be avoided for walking at night. While some areas like Piantini remain relatively safe with good lighting and security, most neighborhoods become more dangerous after sunset with increased crime risk.
tips: Avoid walking alone at night, especially in poorly lit areas or outside tourist zones
Use registered taxis, Uber, or hotel transportation instead of walking at night
Stick to well-frequented restaurants and bars in safe areas like Piantini for nightlife
Don't get extremely intoxicated as it makes you vulnerable to crime
If staying out late, arrange transportation in advance rather than looking for it on the street

transport.metro: Santo Domingo Metro is highly rated for safety, modern, well-maintained, and typically crime-free. It's clean, air-conditioned, affordable at around $0.50 per ride, and operates daily from 6am-10:30pm. The system doesn't cover all tourist areas but is excellent for longer trips between central districts.
transport.rideshare: Uber operates throughout Santo Domingo and is generally safer and more reliable than traditional taxis. It offers fixed app-calculated fares, GPS tracking for security, and eliminates language barriers and fare negotiations. The service is available 24/7 and cars are usually in better condition with working seatbelts and air-conditioning.
transport.taxis: Traditional taxis don't use meters and require fare negotiation before starting your trip. Use reputable companies like Apolo Taxi (popular) or Aero Taxi rather than hailing cabs on the street. Confirm car color and estimated wait time when calling. Cash payment in Dominican Pesos is typically required.
transport.tips: Public transport poses some theft risk, especially at night. The metro is the safest public transport option. Avoid public buses (guaguas) which can be overcrowded and less secure. For safety, use hotel-recommended taxis or ride-share apps.

customs: Greet people with 'Buenos días/tardes/noches' when entering shops or restaurants - politeness is highly valued
Use formal titles like 'Señor' or 'Señora' when addressing older adults or in formal settings
Dress conservatively and neatly in public spaces - appearance matters in Dominican culture and reflects respect
Expect flexible timing for social events ('Dominican time') but arrive punctually for business meetings
Show respect for elders and family hierarchy - offer seats to older people and use polite language
Accept offered food or drinks graciously as refusing without explanation may be seen as rude

health.overview: Healthcare quality varies significantly between private and public facilities. Private hospitals in Santo Domingo like Plaza de la Salud General Hospital and Cedimat Medical Center offer high-quality care with English-speaking staff. Public facilities are well below First World standards and often lack supplies.
health.water: Tap water is not safe to drink. Use bottled or purified water for drinking, brushing teeth, and ice. Most hotels and restaurants provide safe bottled water. Sealed bottles are widely available throughout the city.
health.vaccinations: No special vaccinations required from the US. Routine vaccines should be up to date including COVID-19, flu, hepatitis A. Yellow fever vaccination required if arriving from high-risk countries like Brazil.
health.altitude: Santo Domingo is at sea level with tropical maritime climate. High temperatures and humidity year-round with little seasonal variation. Average temperatures 77-86°F (25-30°C). Hurricane season runs June-November with heaviest rains May-November.

emergency.usEmbassy: US Embassy Santo Domingo: 809-567-7775, located at Av. República de Colombia #57. For after-hours emergencies contact 809-567-7775.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe for tourists?
faq.a: Santo Domingo is moderately safe for tourists who take standard precautions. While it has the highest crime rate in the Dominican Republic, millions visit annually without incidents. Stay in tourist areas, use reliable transport, avoid displaying valuables, and exercise extra caution at night.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe at night?
faq.a: Santo Domingo becomes less safe after dark, especially outside tourist zones. The Zona Colonial empties out around 6pm and walking should be avoided. Use taxis or Uber for nighttime transportation and stick to well-lit, populated areas like Piantini for evening activities.

faq.q: Is Santo Domingo safe for solo female travelers?
faq.a: Solo female travelers can visit safely with extra precautions. Catcalling is extremely common, so dress conservatively and stay alert. Use Uber instead of street taxis, avoid walking alone at night, and trust your instincts about removing yourself from uncomfortable situations.

faq.q: What areas should I avoid in Santo Domingo?
faq.a: Avoid Cristo Rey, Villa Mella, Arroyo Hondo, Los Prados, and San Carlos, especially at night. These areas have higher crime rates including robbery and gang activity. Even 'safer' areas require caution after dark when the city empties out.

faq.q: Is it safe to use public transport in Santo Domingo?
faq.a: The metro system is very safe, modern, and clean - it's the best public transport option. Avoid public buses (guaguas) which pose theft risks. Uber is safer than traditional taxis. If using taxis, call reputable companies like Apolo Taxi rather than hailing on the street.
